summary: This notebook implements a baseline pipeline for mouse behavior detection that explicitly handles variable frame rates through FPS-invariant feature engineering. It demonstrates how to extract spatial-temporal features from tracking coordinates, train an ensemble of tree-based classifiers per action, and apply adaptive temporal thresholding for frame-level localization. The author emphasizes duration-aware window scaling, custom frame-overlap scoring, and robust post-processing to handle overlapping predictions and missing videos. Readers should take away practical techniques for temporal action localization, memory-efficient stratified subsampling, and adaptive thresholding in variable-FPS video data.
competition_flow: Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking files, extracts FPS-scaled spatial-temporal features for single and pair mouse interactions, trains an ensemble of tree models per action, applies adaptive temporal thresholding, and outputs a frame-level action localization submission.
data_reading: Loads per-video tracking data from parquet files
Pivots x/y coordinates by mouse_id and bodypart
Parses behaviors_labeled JSON strings into action labels
data_processing: Handles missing values and reorders DataFrame levels
Splits data into single-mouse and pair-mouse subsets
Applies rolling mean smoothing to prediction probabilities
Filters events shorter than 3 frames as noise
features_engineering: FPS-scaled temporal windows/lags for speed, curvature, turn rate, state transitions, long-range EWM/mean
Relative distance, approach/chase dynamics, velocity alignment/correlation
Lagged displacements, elongation, body angle, and ear convergence features
All temporal parameters are dynamically scaled by video FPS to ensure duration invariance
ensembling: Averages predicted probabilities from an ensemble of LightGBM, XGBoost, and CatBoost classifiers (each wrapped in a stratified subsampling wrapper) trained per action, followed by action/mode-specific adaptive thresholding and temporal smoothing.
insights: Temporal windows and lags must be scaled by the video's FPS to maintain consistent physical durations across videos with different frame rates.
Stratified subsampling helps manage memory and class imbalance during tree model training.
Adaptive per-action thresholds combined with rolling smoothing significantly improve frame-level localization precision.
Custom frame-overlap scoring is necessary because standard metrics do not account for variable-length action spans.
critical_findings: The model explicitly underperforms on 'single' targets, prompting the author to increase training samples for that subset.
Multiple predictions for the same frame from one agent/target pair are treated as errors, and events shorter than 3 frames are filtered as likely noise.
what_did_not_work: The model underperforms on 'single' targets, so the author increased the training sample size for that subset instead of modifying the architecture or features.
